subject: Physics
q: A car is moving on a horizontal curved road with radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate maximum speed of car will be, if friction between tyres and road is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn></math>. [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A car is moving on a horizontal curved road with radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate maximum speed of car will be, if friction between tyres and road is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn></math>. [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
For a car moving with uniform speed along a circular track, the centripetal acceleration is provided by the frictional force acting radially inwards. Therefore, applying Newton's second law along radial direction,

μmg=mv2r

⇒v=μrg

   =0.34×50×10

    ≈13 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the figure, a block of mass 3 kg is kept on a horizontal rough surface of coefficient of friction 133. The critical force to be applied on the vertical surface as shown at an angle 60° with horizontal such that it does not move, will be 3x. The value of x will 

g=10 m s-2; sin60°=32; cos60°=12
question:
As shown in the figure, a block of mass 3 kg is kept on a horizontal rough surface of coefficient of friction 133. The critical force to be applied on the vertical surface as shown at an angle 60° with horizontal such that it does not move, will be 3x. The value of x will 

g=10 m s-2; sin60°=32; cos60°=12
solution:
Fcos60°=μN or F2=133 N         …1

& N=sin60°+3g  …2

From equation 1 & 2

F2=133F32+3g

⇒F=g=10 Newton =3x

So x=103=3.33

question: A body starts from rest on a long inclined plane of slope $45^{\circ}$. The coefficient of friction between the body and the plane varies as $\mu=0.3 x$, where $x$ is distance travelled down the plane. The body will have maximum speed (for $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) when $x=$

subject: Physics
q: The length of a light string is 1.4 m when the tension on it is 5 N. If the tension increases to 7 N , the length of the string is 1.56 m. The original length of the string is _____ m.
question: The length of a light string is 1.4 m when the tension on it is 5 N. If the tension increases to 7 N , the length of the string is 1.56 m. The original length of the string is _____ m.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}\left(\ell-\ell_0\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 5=\mathrm{K}\left(1.4-\ell_0\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 7=\mathrm{K}\left(1.56-\ell_0\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5}{1.4-\ell_0}=\frac{7}{1.56-\ell_0} \\ & \therefore \ell_0=1 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: One end of a massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley $P$ is tied to a hook $C$ while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is $360 \mathrm{~N}$. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) can a man of $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ climb on the rope?
question: One end of a massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley $P$ is tied to a hook $C$ while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is $360 \mathrm{~N}$. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-2}$ ) can a man of $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ climb on the rope?
solution: $\mathrm{T}-60 \mathrm{~g}=60 \mathrm{a} ; \mathrm{T}=3000 \mathrm{~N} ; \quad \therefore \mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$

subject: Physics
q:
The motion of a mass on a spring, with spring constant K is as shown in figure.

The equation of motion is given by, x(t)=Asinωt+Bcosωt with ω=Km.
Suppose that at time t=0, the position of mass is x(0) and velocity v(0), then its displacement can also be represented as x(t)=Ccos(ωt-ϕ), where C and ϕ are
question:
The motion of a mass on a spring, with spring constant K is as shown in figure.

The equation of motion is given by, x(t)=Asinωt+Bcosωt with ω=Km.
Suppose that at time t=0, the position of mass is x(0) and velocity v(0), then its displacement can also be represented as x(t)=Ccos(ωt-ϕ), where C and ϕ are
solution:
x=Asinωt+Bcosωt
v=dxdt=Aωcosωt-Bωsinωt
At t=0, x(0)=B
v(0)=Aω
x=Asinω+Bsinωt+90°

Anet=A2+B2
tanα=BA⇒cotα=AB
⇒x=A2+B2sin(ωt+α)
⇒x=A2+B2cos(ωt-(90-α))

x=Ccos(ωt-ϕ)

⇒C=A2+B2

C=[v(0)]2ω2+[x(0)]2
ϕ=90-α
tanα=cosα=AB

⇒tanϕ=v(0)x(0)·ω
ϕ=tan-1v(0)x(0)ω

subject: Physics
q: A spring of force constant $15 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is cut into two pieces. If the ratio of their length is $1: 3$, then the force constant of smaller piece is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$.
question: A spring of force constant $15 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is cut into two pieces. If the ratio of their length is $1: 3$, then the force constant of smaller piece is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$.
solution:
Spring force constant is inversely proportional to length: $k \propto \frac{1}{L}$
So $kL = $ constant for a given spring material and structure.
Original spring: $k = 15$ N/m, length $= L$.
Smaller piece has length $\frac{L}{4}$ (since length ratio is 1:3, smaller is $\frac{1}{4}$ of total).
For smaller piece: $k_1 \times \frac{L}{4} = 15 \times L$
$k_1 = 15 \times 4 = 60$ N/m

subject: Physics
q: A small block starts slipping down from a point B on an inclined plane AB, which is making an angle θ with the horizontal section BC is smooth and the remaining section CA is rough with a coefficient of friction μ. It is found that the block comes to rest as it reaches the bottom (point A) of the inclined plane. If BC=2AC, the coefficient of friction is given by μ=k tanθ. The value of k is .......
question: A small block starts slipping down from a point B on an inclined plane AB, which is making an angle θ with the horizontal section BC is smooth and the remaining section CA is rough with a coefficient of friction μ. It is found that the block comes to rest as it reaches the bottom (point A) of the inclined plane. If BC=2AC, the coefficient of friction is given by μ=k tanθ. The value of k is .......
solution:
Let AC=ℓ  ∴  BC=2ℓ  ∴  AB=3ℓ

Apply work – Energy theorem

Wt+Wmg=ΔKE

mg(3ℓ)sinθ-μmgcosθ(ℓ)=0+0

μmgcosθℓ=3mg/sinθ

μ=3tanθ=ktanθ

k=3

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is tied to a spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, while the other end of spring is fixed at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math>. If the body moves about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> in a circular path on a smooth horizontal surface with constant angular speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the ratio of extension in the spring to its natural length will be :
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is tied to a spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, while the other end of spring is fixed at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math>. If the body moves about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> in a circular path on a smooth horizontal surface with constant angular speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the ratio of extension in the spring to its natural length will be :
solution:
Let the extension in spring be x and the natural length of spring be l.

Here the centrifugal force will stretch the spring.

So, centrifugal force=spring force

kx=ml+xω2⇒12.5x=15l+x25⇒1.5x=l⇒xl=11.5=23

⇒x:l=2:3

subject: Physics
q: A bag is gently dropped on a conveyor belt moving at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The coefficient of friction between the conveyor belt and bag is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> Initially, the bag slips on the belt before it stops due to friction. The distance travelled by the bag on the belt during slipping motion is : [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A bag is gently dropped on a conveyor belt moving at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The coefficient of friction between the conveyor belt and bag is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> Initially, the bag slips on the belt before it stops due to friction. The distance travelled by the bag on the belt during slipping motion is : [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
When the bag is dropped on the conveyor belt, the acceleration of the bag will be a=μg=4 m s-2

The bag will stop moving on the conveyor when the velocity of the bag and the velocity of the conveyor becomes equal.

u=0, v=2 m s-1

Using the equation of motion for constant acceleration,

v2=u2+2as

⇒s=0.5 m

subject: Physics
q: Consider a car moving on a straight road with a speed of $100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The distance at which car can be stopped is $\left[\mu_{\mathrm{k}}=0.5\right]$
question: Consider a car moving on a straight road with a speed of $100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The distance at which car can be stopped is $\left[\mu_{\mathrm{k}}=0.5\right]$
solution:
$\mu_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{mgs}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^2$
$\mathrm{~s}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{2 \mu_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{g}}=1000 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: In the given arrangement of a doubly inclined plane two blocks of masses M and m are placed. The blocks are connected by a light string passing over an ideal pulley as shown. The coefficient of friction between the surface of the plane and the blocks is 0.25. The value of m, for which M=10 kg will move down with an acceleration of 2 m s-2, is: (take g=10 m s-2 and tan 37°=34)
question: In the given arrangement of a doubly inclined plane two blocks of masses M and m are placed. The blocks are connected by a light string passing over an ideal pulley as shown. The coefficient of friction between the surface of the plane and the blocks is 0.25. The value of m, for which M=10 kg will move down with an acceleration of 2 m s-2, is: (take g=10 m s-2 and tan 37°=34)
solution:
For M block:

10gsin53°-μ(10g) cos53°-T=10×2

⇒T=80-15-20

⇒T=45 N

For m block:

T–mg sin 37°-μ mg cos 37°=m×2

⇒45=10 m

⇒m=4.5 kg

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> slides down on a rough inclined plane with constant velocity. The angle made by the incline plane with horizontal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The magnitude of the contact force will be :
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> slides down on a rough inclined plane with constant velocity. The angle made by the incline plane with horizontal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The magnitude of the contact force will be :
solution:
As the block moves with constant velocity, so force on the block should be balanced. The forces acting on the block are its weight, normal contact force and friction. The contact force is vector sum of normal contact and friction. As the forces are balanced, the contact force should be equal and opposite to its weight.

∴Contact force =Mg

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> moves along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis such that it's velocity varies with displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> the force acting on the body is:
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> moves along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis such that it's velocity varies with displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> the force acting on the body is:
solution:
It is given that 

 v=10x m s-1

Force is defined by F=mdvdt   ...(i)

Multiplying and dividing equation (i) by dx

F=mdvdtdxdx⇒F=mdxdtdvdx=mvdvdx   ...(ii)

Finding the values of the terms in equation (ii)
dvdx=102x=5x
vdvdx=a=10x×5x=50 m s-2

Hence, the force is 
F=12×50=25 N.

subject: Physics
q: Two blocks  A and  B of masses mA=1 kg and mB=3 kg are kept on the table as shown in figure. The coefficients of friction between A and B is 0.2 and between  B and the surface of the table is also 0.2. The maximum force F that can be applied on B horizontally, so that the block  A does not slide over the block B is : [Take  g=10 m/s2 ]
question: Two blocks  A and  B of masses mA=1 kg and mB=3 kg are kept on the table as shown in figure. The coefficients of friction between A and B is 0.2 and between  B and the surface of the table is also 0.2. The maximum force F that can be applied on B horizontally, so that the block  A does not slide over the block B is : [Take  g=10 m/s2 ]
solution:
Maximum possible acceleration of 1 kg block a=µmgm=µg=2 m/s2

For 1 g block not to slide the maximum acceleration of both blocks should be

Fmax-µmg=mamax
Fmax-0.2×4×10=4×2
Fmax- 8=8
Fmax=16 N

subject: Physics
q: The time taken by an object to slide down <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> rough inclined plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> incline plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the incline plane is:
question: The time taken by an object to slide down <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> rough inclined plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> incline plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the incline plane is:
solution:
Let angle of inclination is θ=45°.

Then, acceleration along the incline in case of smooth incline will be, a1=g sinθ=g2.

In case of rough incline, kinetic friction will act against the direction of motion. Normal force acting on the object will be mgcosθ. Therefore, acceleration along the incline in case of rough incline will be,

a2=g sinθ-μg cosθ=g2-μg2.

Now as per question,

t2=nt1

and as the displacement covered is the same in both case, 0+12a1t12=0+12a2t22

⇒g2t12=g2-μg2n2t12

⇒μ=1-1n2

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> electric motor lifts an elevator having a maximum total load capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the frictional force on the elevator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the speed of the elevator at full load is close to : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>HP</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>746</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> electric motor lifts an elevator having a maximum total load capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the frictional force on the elevator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the speed of the elevator at full load is close to : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>HP</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>746</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
4000×V+mg×V=P
60×7464000+20000=V
V=1.9 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
A vertical closed cylinder is separated into two parts by a frictionless piston of mass m and of negligible thickness. The piston is free to move along the length of the cylinder. The length of the cylinder above piston is l1, and that below the piston is l2, such that l1>l2. Each part of the cylinder contains n moles of an ideal gas at equal temperature T. If the piston is stationary, its mass m will be given by:

(R is universal gas constant and g is the acceleration due to gravity)
question:
A vertical closed cylinder is separated into two parts by a frictionless piston of mass m and of negligible thickness. The piston is free to move along the length of the cylinder. The length of the cylinder above piston is l1, and that below the piston is l2, such that l1>l2. Each part of the cylinder contains n moles of an ideal gas at equal temperature T. If the piston is stationary, its mass m will be given by:

(R is universal gas constant and g is the acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
P1+mgA=P2

⇒nRTl1A+mgA=nRTl2A

⇒m=nRTg1l2-1l1

=nRTgl1-l2l1l2

subject: Physics
q: A small block of mass $m$ slides down from the top of a frictionless inclined surface, while the inclined plane is moving towards left with constant acceleration $a_{0}$. The angle between the inclined plane and ground is $\theta$ and its base length is $L$. Assuming that initially the small block is at the top of the inclined plane, the time it takes to reach the lowest point of the inclined plane is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A small block of mass $m$ slides down from the top of a frictionless inclined surface, while the inclined plane is moving towards left with constant acceleration $a_{0}$. The angle between the inclined plane and ground is $\theta$ and its base length is $L$. Assuming that initially the small block is at the top of the inclined plane, the time it takes to reach the lowest point of the inclined plane is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$mg\sin\theta - ma_0\cos\theta = ma$
$a = g\sin\theta - a_0\cos\theta$
Now using,
$S = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}a_{down}t^2$
$\dfrac{L}{\cos\theta} = \dfrac{1}{2}(g\sin\theta - a_0\cos\theta)t^2$
$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2L}{g\sin\theta\cos\theta - a_0\cos^2\theta}}$
$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4L}{g\sin 2\theta - a_0(1 + \cos 2\theta)}}$

subject: Physics
q: A string of length $L$ is fixed at one end and carries a mass of $M$ at the other end. The mass makes $\left(\frac{3}{\pi}\right)$ rotations per second about the vertical axis passing through end of the string as shown. The tension in the string is $\ldots\ldots$ ML.
question: A string of length $L$ is fixed at one end and carries a mass of $M$ at the other end. The mass makes $\left(\frac{3}{\pi}\right)$ rotations per second about the vertical axis passing through end of the string as shown. The tension in the string is $\ldots\ldots$ ML.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\frac{3}{\pi} \times 2 \pi=6 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\ & R=L \sin \theta\end{aligned}$
and $T=M \sqrt{g^2+\omega^4 R^2}$ Also, $T \sin \theta=M \omega^2 \cdot L \sin \theta$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad T=M(36) \mathrm{L} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad T=36 \mathrm{ML} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is suspended by a rope of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, from the ceiling. A force <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is applied horizontally at the mid-point of the rope such that the top half of the rope makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the vertical. Then <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> equals: (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the rope to be massless)
question: A mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is suspended by a rope of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, from the ceiling. A force <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is applied horizontally at the mid-point of the rope such that the top half of the rope makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the vertical. Then <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> equals: (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the rope to be massless)
solution:
Let the tension in the string is T.

Applying the condition of equilibrium in the vertical direction,

Tcos45°=100

⇒ T2=100   ...1

In the horizontal direction,

Tsin45°=F

⇒ T2=F

Put the value of T from equation 1,

⇒ F=100 N

So, the horizontal force applied on the rope will be 100 N.

subject: Physics
q: A conical pendulum of length l makes an angle θ=45° with respect to Z-axis and moves in a circle in the XY plane. The radius of the circle is 0.4 m and its center is vertically below O. The speed of the pendulum, in its circular path, will be - Take g=10 m s-2
question: A conical pendulum of length l makes an angle θ=45° with respect to Z-axis and moves in a circle in the XY plane. The radius of the circle is 0.4 m and its center is vertically below O. The speed of the pendulum, in its circular path, will be - Take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Tsin⁡θ=mv2r

Tcos⁡θ=mg

tan⁡θ=v2rg

θ=45o

v2=rg

v=rg=0.4×10

v=2 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ under the action of constant force $\vec{F}=F_x \hat{i}+F_y \hat{j}$ has velocity at $\mathrm{t}=0 \mathrm{~s}$ as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s})$ and at $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{~s}$ as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. The force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ is:
question: A body of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ under the action of constant force $\vec{F}=F_x \hat{i}+F_y \hat{j}$ has velocity at $\mathrm{t}=0 \mathrm{~s}$ as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s})$ and at $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{~s}$ as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. The force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ is:
solution:
From question,
Mass of body, $m=5 \mathrm{~kg}$
Velocity at $t=0$,
$$
u=(6 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
Velocity at $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$,
$$
v=+6 \hat{j} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
Force, $F=$ ?
Acceleration, $a=\frac{v-u}{t}$
$$
=\frac{6 \hat{j}-(6 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j})}{10}=\frac{-3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}}{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
$$
Force, $F=m a$
$$
=5 \times \frac{(-3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j})}{5}=(-3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) N
$$

subject: Physics
q: Three blocks $M_1, M_2, M_3$ having masses $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively are hanging from a smooth pully using rope 1,2 and 3 as shown in figure. The tension in the rope $1, T_1$ when they are moving upward with acceleration of $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ is _____$\mathrm{N}\left(\right.$ if $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ).
question: Three blocks $M_1, M_2, M_3$ having masses $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively are hanging from a smooth pully using rope 1,2 and 3 as shown in figure. The tension in the rope $1, T_1$ when they are moving upward with acceleration of $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ is _____$\mathrm{N}\left(\right.$ if $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ).
solution:
FBD of $M_1$ :
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_1-200=(4+6+10) \times 2 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}_1=240 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The machine as shown has 2 rods of length 1 m connected by a pivot at the top. The end of one rod is connected to the floor by a stationary pivot and the end of the other rod has roller that rolls along the floor in a slot. As the roller goes back and forth, a 2 kg weight moves up and down. If the roller is moving towards right at a constant speed, the weight moves up with a :
question: The machine as shown has 2 rods of length 1 m connected by a pivot at the top. The end of one rod is connected to the floor by a stationary pivot and the end of the other rod has roller that rolls along the floor in a slot. As the roller goes back and forth, a 2 kg weight moves up and down. If the roller is moving towards right at a constant speed, the weight moves up with a :
options: Speed which is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of that of the roller when the weight is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground
solution:
vA=dxdt=d2lcosθdt=-2lsinθdθdt and 

Vertical velocity of B, vB=dydt=dlsinθdt=lcosθdθdt

vAvB=-2tanθ⇒vB=-vAcotθ2 
 
θ→increase

cotθ→decrease

Hence, vB→decrease.

q: A force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>40</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts on a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. question: A force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>40</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts on a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. If the body starts from rest, its position vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be
solution:
dv→dt=a→=F→m=(8i^+2j^) m s-2

dr→dt=v→=(8ti^+2tj^) m s-1

r→=(i^+2j^)t22 m

At t=10 sec

r→=[(8i^+2j^)50] m

⇒r→=(400i^+100j^) m

subject: Physics
q:
A wedge $Y$ with mass of $10$ kg and all frictionless surfaces and the inclined surface making $37°$ with horizontal. A block $X$ with mass $2$ kg is placed at the highest point of the wedge as shown in figure is at rest. At $t = 0$ wedge $(Y)$ is pulled toward right with constant force $(f)$ of $24$ N. Taking the block $X$ at rest at $t = 0$, the time taken by it to slide down $8.8$ m on the slope, while $Y$ is on the move, is _____ s.
(take $\tan(37°) = 3/4$ and $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question:
A wedge $Y$ with mass of $10$ kg and all frictionless surfaces and the inclined surface making $37°$ with horizontal. A block $X$ with mass $2$ kg is placed at the highest point of the wedge as shown in figure is at rest. At $t = 0$ wedge $(Y)$ is pulled toward right with constant force $(f)$ of $24$ N. Taking the block $X$ at rest at $t = 0$, the time taken by it to slide down $8.8$ m on the slope, while $Y$ is on the move, is _____ s.
(take $\tan(37°) = 3/4$ and $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Horizontal acceleration of the wedge (assuming the block and wedge move together horizontally):
$A = \dfrac{f}{M + m} = \dfrac{24}{10 + 2} = 2\text{ m/s}^2$

Working in the non-inertial frame of the wedge, the block experiences a pseudo force $mA$ directed opposite to the wedge's acceleration. The components along the incline are:
Component of gravity down the incline: $g\sin 37^\circ$
Component of pseudo force up the incline: $A\cos 37^\circ$

Net relative acceleration of the block down the incline:
$a_{\text{rel}} = g\sin 37^\circ - A\cos 37^\circ$

Using $\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$ and $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$:
$a_{\text{rel}} = 10(0.6) - 2(0.8) = 6 - 1.6 = 4.4\text{ m/s}^2$

Time taken to slide a distance of $8.8\text{ m}$ along the incline, starting from rest:
$s = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{\text{rel}}\, t^2$

$8.8 = \dfrac{1}{2}(4.4)\,t^2$

$8.8 = 2.2\,t^2$

$t^2 = 4 \Rightarrow t = 2\text{ s}$

Hence, the required time is $2\text{ s}$.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is fixed to one end of a light spring having force constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and unstretched length <math><mi>l</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The other end is fixed. The system is given an angular speed <math><mi>ω</mi></math> about the fixed end of the spring such that it rotates in a circle in gravity free space. Then the stretch in the spring is:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is fixed to one end of a light spring having force constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and unstretched length <math><mi>l</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The other end is fixed. The system is given an angular speed <math><mi>ω</mi></math> about the fixed end of the spring such that it rotates in a circle in gravity free space. Then the stretch in the spring is:
solution:
As we know, the spring force will give the necessary centripetal force for rotation.

So, 

mω2l+x=kx
lx+1=kmω2

 Thus, the stretch in the spring
x=lmω2k-mω2

subject: Physics
q: A tube of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is filled completely with an incompressible liquid of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and closed at both ends. The tube is then rotated in horizontal plane about one of its ends with a uniform angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mi>F</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> be the force exerted by the liquid at the other end then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: A tube of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is filled completely with an incompressible liquid of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and closed at both ends. The tube is then rotated in horizontal plane about one of its ends with a uniform angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mi>F</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> be the force exerted by the liquid at the other end then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
Considering an element of tube of length dx having mass dm at a distance of x from right end as shown in figure below.

Now, force acting of that element is F=∫dmω2x

Integrating the above relation for whole length of tube, we have 

 F=∫0LmLdxω2x

=mLω2L22 =mω2L2

⇒ω=2mLF  =20.25×0.5F

=16F=4F rad s-1

Hence, the value of x=4.

subject: Physics
q: At $t=0$, a body of mass $100$ g starts moving under the influence of a force $(5\hat{i}+10\hat{j})$ N. After $2$ s its position is $(2x\hat{i}+5y\hat{j})$ m. The ratio $x:y$ is ______.
question: At $t=0$, a body of mass $100$ g starts moving under the influence of a force $(5\hat{i}+10\hat{j})$ N. After $2$ s its position is $(2x\hat{i}+5y\hat{j})$ m. The ratio $x:y$ is ______.
solution:
Mass $m = 100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}$

Force $\vec{F} = 5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} \text{ N}$

Acceleration $\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j}}{0.1} = 50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j} \text{ m/s}^2$

Since the body starts from rest, initial velocity $\vec{u} = 0$.

Position after $t = 2 \text{ s}$ is given by $\vec{r} = \vec{u}t + \dfrac{1}{2}\vec{a}t^2$

$\vec{r} = \dfrac{1}{2}(50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j})(2)^2 = 100\hat{i} + 200\hat{j} \text{ m}$

Comparing with the given position $\vec{r} = 2x\hat{i} + 5y\hat{j}$:

$2x = 100 \Rightarrow x = 50$

$5y = 200 \Rightarrow y = 40$

The ratio $x:y = 50:40 = 5:4$

Answer: $5:4$

subject: Physics
q: An object with mass 500 g moves along x -axis with speed $v=4 \sqrt{x} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The force acting on the object is :
question: An object with mass 500 g moves along x -axis with speed $v=4 \sqrt{x} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The force acting on the object is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & F=M \times a \\ & v=4 \sqrt{x} \\ & v^2=16 x \\ & 2 v \frac{d v}{d x}=16 \\ & \frac{v d v}{d x}=\frac{16}{2}=8 \\ & F=0.5 \times 8=4 N\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is moving in a straight line with momentum p. Starting at time $t=0,$ a force $F=k$ t acts in the same direction on the moving particle during time interval T so that its momentum changes from $\mathrm{p}$ to $3 \mathrm{p}$. Here $k$ is a constant. The value of $\mathrm{T}$ is
question: A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is moving in a straight line with momentum p. Starting at time $t=0,$ a force $F=k$ t acts in the same direction on the moving particle during time interval T so that its momentum changes from $\mathrm{p}$ to $3 \mathrm{p}$. Here $k$ is a constant. The value of $\mathrm{T}$ is
solution:
From Newton's second law

$\frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{kt}$

Integrating both sides we get, $\int_{\mathrm{p}}^{3 \mathrm{p}} \mathrm{dp}=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{ktdt} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{p}]_{\mathrm{p}}^{3 \mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{k}\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{T}}$

$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathrm{kT}^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=2 \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{k}}}$

subject: Physics
q: The position vector of a particle related to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>t</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>15</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The direction of net force experienced by the particle is :
question: The position vector of a particle related to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>t</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>15</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The direction of net force experienced by the particle is :
solution:
Given, the position vector of the particle is

r→=10ti^+15t2j^+7k^

The velocity of the particle can be calculated as follows:

v→=dr→dt=ddt10ti^+15t2j^+7k^= 10i^+30tj^

And, the acceleration of the particle is given by

a→=dv→dt=ddt10i^+30tj^=30j^

Hence, the force on the particle can be written as

F→=ma→=30mj^

q: A uniform cylinder of length L and mass M having cross-sectional area A is suspended, with its length vertical, from a fixed point by a massless spring, such that it is half submerged in a liquid of density σ at equilibrium position. The extension x 0 of the spring when it is in equilibrium is :
question: A uniform cylinder of length L and mass M having cross-sectional area A is suspended, with its length vertical, from a fixed point by a massless spring, such that it is half submerged in a liquid of density σ at equilibrium position. The extension x 0 of the spring when it is in equilibrium is :
solution:
kx 0 + F B = mg

kx 0 + σ L 2 Ag = Mg


x 0 = Mg - σ LAg 2 k

    = Mg k 1 - σ LA 2 M
